title: A Mehxico Story
body:
Mexico had the honour of opening the festival of football and, in true Roman style, they needed a sacrifice. The hapless lambs were South Africa. Every World Cup I find myself wondering whether this will be the year African nations finally show their strength and depth. Unfortunately, if South Africa are anything to go by, the answer remains a firm no.

They looked out of their depth from the first whistle and Mexico should have been several up by half time. Against better opposition, El Tri might have rued those missed chances — but South Africa never really looked like making a game of it. A second goal in the second half, bookended by a flurry of red cards (three in total, a World Cup opening game record), made the second period watchable if not exactly a classic. Mexico cruised.

title: Good Korea, Bad Korea
body:
I only caught the highlights of the day's second game — which may become something of a recurring theme — but it looked like another dominant first half from the nominal home side. South Korea racked up almost as many missed chances as there were empty seats in Guadalajara (also likely to be a recurring theme), and went in at the break with nothing to show for it.

The second half started brightly for South Korea, but Czech Republic somehow fashioned a goal against the run of play. Luckily for those of you with the good Korea in your picks, they levelled things up before substitute Oh Hyeon-gyu popped up to secure the winner in the closing stages. A Soucek header that briefly threatened to nick it back for the Czechs was ruled out for offside, and that was your lot.

title: No They Can'tada
body:
Day two saw our remaining hosts get their tournaments underway with mixed results.

Canada went behind early and spent the rest of the game trying to claw it back. Throughout, they looked the side more intent on actually playing football, while Bosnia and Herzegovina were perfectly happy to sit back, soak up pressure, and wait for something to happen on the break. It was exactly one of those breaks that saw them burst clear and take the lead.

Canada continued to push, but their waves of attack kept crashing against a wall of defenders and rarely troubled the opposition's box.

The second half was more of the same — Canada creating, BiH sneaking. Canada's equaliser with ten minutes remaining felt like it would set up a breathless finale, but although both sides had chances, a draw felt like the right result after an entertaining match.

title: Surprisingly Fine
body:
South of the (Canadian) border, in the hallowed land of Soccer, the USA started with high expectations — and after this display, those expectations might actually be warranted. Their first half against Paraguay was some of the best football I've seen from the US team, and their 3–0 lead at the break felt thoroughly deserved.

Their opener — seven minutes in — was a horrible poke-in from a Paraguay defender, but it came at the end of some lovely build-up play. By the time Balogun added his second deep into first-half injury time, the US could stroll into the dressing room feeling very pleased with themselves.

The second half wasn't as convincing. The US eased off, made a few more mistakes, and some frailties at the back emerged that a better team might look to exploit. Paraguay did find the net with a well-worked goal the US defence should have dealt with more comfortably — but it was only a small speed bump on the road to a well-won victory. A fourth goal just before the final whistle was the bow on top.
footer: A personal footnote: while watching the 2002 World Cup with an American girl I was trying to impress, I made the drunken prediction that the US would reach a World Cup final within 25 years. This is — somewhat alarmingly — that window's last chance. I don't think it's going to happen. But they do look closer than I've seen them before.

title: A Match Worth Waiting For?
body:
As the media has frequently reminded us, it's been 28 years since Scotland made it to a World Cup. For Haiti the wait has been twice as long, and from the first whistle they were keen to make clear they hadn't travelled all this way just to be a footnote.

Every chance they got, Haiti came flying forward in numbers, with Scotland working hard to hold off the cavalry charge.

Scotland had their chances — not as many as they'd have liked — and it ultimately came down to a scuffed McGinn shot that deflected past the keeper to put them ahead. A goal on the board settled Scotland's nerves somewhat, though Haiti continued to threaten an equaliser right to the end.

Desperate for a cushion, Scotland probed for a second, but poor finishing and dogged defending kept Haiti in it until the final whistle.

Three points from their opening game was the bare minimum Scotland needed if they're to have any hope of reaching the knockouts. Morocco and Brazil are considerably sterner tests, and they're going to need to play a good deal better than this to squeeze out results that could get them through the group stage.

title: Scoring Is All That Matters
body:
Australia are one of those sides that always seems to promise a lot at World Cups and rarely delivers. Turkey were one of the teams I thought might outperform their PickerGame cost. We've established what I know.

Turkey applied pressure for 90 minutes. They had most of the ball, most of the chances... and Australia scored twice. And that, as they say, is how you take the points.

In a game that will not trouble the history books, a couple of individual moments separated two sides unlikely to trouble the sharp end of the knockout stages. Turkey will need to be more ruthless in front of goal. Australia will need to do, well, more — but they'll take it.

title: A Close Shave
body:
When I looked at the match schedule, the first fixture that leapt out at me was Brazil vs Morocco. A meeting between arguably the best sides from South America and Africa — hopefully a feast of football.

Out of the blocks it was Morocco who looked the sharper side. They had several chances and it was no surprise when a great through ball and a deft dink put them one up.

Going behind gave Brazil a nudge, though they still needed a moment of individual brilliance from Vinícius Jr. to claw their way back into it.

Brazil would have been relieved to go in level at the break, but any hopes of a change in fortune in the second half were swiftly dashed. Both sides made changes, Morocco continued to look the better team, and they'll feel they deserved more than a point — though a draw against the tournament favourites is a decent return. Brazil will be sending Vini Jr. and Alisson a very large fruit basket and quietly hoping the rest of the squad turns up for the next one.

title: Persistence Pays
body:
I've been saying in the build-up to this World Cup that Qatar would do better than expected. Well, even a broken cuckoo clock is right twice a day.

Not taking your chances has been a theme of this tournament already. While Canada had to settle for a point, Switzerland drawing with Qatar is the first genuine upset of the competition.

The Swiss had already squandered several clear chances before being awarded a penalty that wasn't — or at least shouldn't have been. It was well struck, but I'm going to need more proof it wasn't offside before I move on.

Switzerland continued to spurn chances for the remainder of the game, always looking the better side but never quite looking like they actually wanted to win it.

What Qatar lacked in talent they made up for in sheer bloody-mindedness — though it never really felt like it would pay off. When Lee Dixon said, two minutes into injury time, that there was still time for Qatar to grab a point, I filed it under "things pundits say." Reader, he was right.

With less than two minutes of injury time remaining, Qatar were on the board and seconds away from their first ever World Cup point. The Swiss finally found second gear. Too little, too late.

title: One Nil to the Team That Scored
body:
This was a game between two teams who felt defending was beneath them and shots on target were against the spirit of the game. For the majority of the ninety minutes both sides seemed perfectly happy to break with pace and then put the final shot wide.
Ecuador started the stronger but Ivory Coast soon found their way into the game.
One goal was always going to be enough to steal the points, and in the end it was Ivory Coast who managed to find the net — though "find the net" perhaps flatters both teams given the afternoon they'd had.

title: A View From The Sofa (Highlights Only)
body:
I turned on my TV at 8pm this evening, ready to watch Germany humble one of the many teams who should probably be at home watching. Thanks to the frankly baffling match schedule, all I got was adverts followed by the build-up to the next game.

So this is another match report based on ten minutes of highlights. Unlike the middle-of-the-night games — which no one watches and I can embellish freely — I have to be at least vaguely careful not to just make things up with this one.

This was always going to be one-sided, and when Germany went one up from a great strike six minutes in, expectations were duly set.
When Curaçao equalised, it felt like an M. Night Shyamalan twist — although much earlier in the story than usual. Were we about to see a repeat of Germany's group stage collapses from the last two World Cups?

Unfortunately for the tiny island nation, the celebrations for their first ever World Cup goal weren't destined to last. Germany were back on script with two goals before half time and another within minutes of the restart.

As the goals continued to flow, Curaçao fashioned a few chances but were never really going to get back into it. Hopefully, they can add to their tally before what looks likely to be a brief tournament run comes to an end.

For Germany, things can only get harder — but they'll be glad to have started strong and look every inch one of the favourites to be there when it matters.

title: Shower Timing: A Cautionary Tale
body:
The first 50 minutes of this clash weren't the most riveting football of the tournament. As it was getting late I decided to quickly jump in the shower before catching the last thirty minutes. Unfortunately this meant I missed all three goals.

The thing I find most surprising about the hydration breaks is that we just get to watch players drinking water. Surely they could at least show us a mini highlights package. Or failing that, some adverts. At least then I'd know what car I should be driving.

Netherlands are one of those teams who seem like they should be a footballing giant, but who always end up underperforming. Like Chelsea. Japan feel like they should be the pride of Asian football, but I'm not sure where that pride should rank in the world game.
Both teams should make it through to the knockouts but will be hoping for better performances.

Ok, I'll admit it. I was keen to get to bed so wrote this with 15 minutes left on the clock hoping for an easy life. Japan deserved that equaliser and hopefully that's the last time I need to update this.

title: Flat Pack Football
body:
Sweden are one of those teams I feel should do well but never quite expect to reach the semifinals. Tunisia I'll confess I know nothing about, so I genuinely can't tell you whether this scoreline was a shock or whether Sweden simply played a blinder.

Tunisia had some good attacks and deserved to get on the scoresheet, but even at 2-1 this never really looked like finishing in anything other than a Swedish win. Their front line looks sharp and I think they're going to punish any team that can't get their defence in order.

This was always going to be a tough group and I suspect Tunisia will struggle to pick up much from their remaining games.
